topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the application form, the crisp white paper feeling strangely mocking in his hands. He reread the job description for the marketing coordinator role for the third time, his brow furrowed deep enough to create canyons between his eyebrows. Beside him, Maya hummed a cheerful, off-key tune, tapping her pen against her chin with an air of complete confidence. "Come on, Liam," she chirped, not looking up. "You’re brilliant at the creative side. This is your shot." He shifted in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight. He traced the lines of the required experience, a knot tightening in his stomach. *It’s just a formality,* he thought, though the knot only seemed to pull tighter. He felt a distinct urge to question the entire premise of the competition, a low, simmering dissatisfaction bubbling beneath his calm exterior.

[story 2]

"Seriously, Maya? You're applying for the same position?" Ben muttered, leaning back so abruptly his chair scraped loudly against the floor. He watched her meticulously highlight bullet points on her resume, her movements precise and utterly self-assured. He crossed his arms tightly over his chest, a slight tremor running through his hands. He felt a distinct urge to find a flaw, a tiny inconsistency that would unravel her whole presentation. "It seems... convenient," he said, his voice flat, deliberately devoid of warmth. Maya finally looked up, a bright, almost challenging smile on her face. "Convenient? Or maybe just smart planning?" she countered, her tone light as air, which only made Ben feel colder. He just shook his head slowly, the movement heavy with unspoken doubt.

[story 3]

I picked up the cover sheet, the ink seeming to mock my efforts. The listing for the project manager position was exactly what I’d been aiming for, yet seeing Maya’s name already filed in the shared drive made my jaw clench. I chewed on the end of my pen, the plastic taste metallic on my tongue. She was so eager, so brimming with that easy, undeniable self-belief. I watched her pace the small coffee shop table, her energy practically vibrating off her. *She’s going to nail it,* I told myself, though the thought felt flimsy, like thin ice over deep water. I focused instead on the fine print, searching for any loophole, any tiny detail that might betray a weakness. A persistent sense of unease settled in my chest, a heavy, unwelcome weight that made breathing feel shallow.
